what_it_does: A standalone design-system specimen page that visually demonstrates the system's 'functional spine': canonical device surfaces (desktop/tablet/mobile portrait/landscape mockup frames), the fluid type scale, the 'nothing teleports' motion language (animated tiles), the elevation ramp, and an overflow-safe pill group. Links ../styles.css for tokens and uses a small inline script to build the motion tiles and trigger their replay on hover/load.
observations: Opens with an HTML comment directive @dsCard group="Spacing" name/subtitle/viewport — metadata consumed by an external gallery/card harness.
Links ../styles.css and styles every element via CSS custom properties (var(--zone-base-surface), --ink, --font-body, --fs-h2, --elev-2, --accent-gold, etc.) rather than hardcoded values.
Renders four device frames at captioned dimensions (desktop 1440x900, tablet 834x1112, mobile portrait 390x844, mobile landscape 844x390).
Motion section is data-driven from a JS array MO with classes enter-up/down/left/scale and easing tokens (--ease-entrance/exit/emphasized/spring), replaying on mouseenter and staggered on load.
Elevation ramp shows e0..e5 plus a glow-active state; uses utility-looking classes (.e0../.glow-active) that must be defined in styles.css.
Includes an 'overflow-safe pill group' demo with an intentionally over-long label to test wrapping/truncation.
A few literal values appear in the local <style> (#fff backgrounds, fixed px widths/heights for the demo frames) alongside the token references.
standouts: The motion tiles are interactive (hover-to-replay, staggered auto-play) — a live demonstration rather than a static swatch.
Deliberate stress-test case: a pill labelled 'this label is intentionally far too long to fit' to prove overflow handling.
conventions: Follows the specimen/card pattern: @dsCard comment header + self-contained HTML linking the shared stylesheet.
References design tokens via var() for nearly all colour/type/elevation values (the single-source discipline).
Section headers use the recurring h2 treatment (uppercase bronze label with a rotated gold gem ::before).
Uses #fff and fixed px in local demo styles — minor literals inside a specimen, consistent with other specimens in the set.
concerns: Relies on token/utility classes (e0..e5, glow-active, enter-*, pill-group, --vi-strong, --zone-panel-strong) defined elsewhere in styles.css; if those names drift this page silently shows wrong/blank states (var fallbacks are mostly absent).
notes: A self-contained gallery specimen proving the system's surfaces, fluid type, motion easings, and elevation ramp work from tokens — useful as both a visual reference and a smoke-test of the stylesheet's named tokens/utilities.
questions: Are the easing/elevation/zone utility class names used here (enter-up, glow-active, e0..e5, --vi-strong) guaranteed to exist in styles.css, or could this specimen render incompletely if a token is renamed?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the subtitle promises multi-surface frames, fluid type, and the motion language, and the page demonstrates exactly those plus elevation and an overflow case.
intention_signals: Trying to make the abstract token system tangible to the eye — a single page where someone can see the surfaces, type scale, motion feel, and elevation depth working together and replay the motions.
novelty: The interactive, replayable motion tiles and the intentional overflow stress-case make this more than a passive swatch sheet.
contribution: Contributes a live visual+interactive specimen of the foundational dimensions (surfaces, fluid type, motion easings, elevation, overflow safety) bound to the shared tokens — a renderable reference and smoke-test of those tokens.
summary_for_embedding: A ConceptV design-system specimen HTML page demonstrating canonical device surfaces, the fluid type scale, the 'nothing teleports' motion easings (interactive tiles), the elevation ramp, and an overflow-safe pill group, all driven by shared CSS tokens.
intention_for_embedding: To visually and interactively prove the system's foundational surfaces, type, motion, and depth tokens render correctly and feel right to the eye.
